CURRENT AFFAIRS,

In our issue of the 2nd inst. we referred to thoe va-
cancy for a head master to the new Technical Schools
in Waterford. The vacancy was advertised in Britain,
and there were about 100 applicants for it. Tt will be
remembered that Sir Horace Plunkett, who is now un-
dergoing a process of summor whitewashing, said ab the

- Inquiry that most of the aliens for the appointment of
which the Department was “abused” were appointed
by the local representative bodies, the Department
merely passing on their qualifications. 'Was not that
very plausible? Sir Horace is nothing if not plausiblo,
and it is no wonder he has a “ way with him ’’ for deal-
ing with mixed committees and boards. Now, in the
matter of the Waterford affair, all the names of the ap-
plicants were sent to the Department, and the Depart-
ment’s office was to select five out of the number for

* submission to the final verdict of the Waterford Techni-
cal Committee. If the Waterford Technical Commit-
teo appointed a Scotchman, while a competent Irishman
was available, Sir Horaco would plausibly round off
any “ abuse ’’ that might result by blandly stating that

it was the local committee that made tho appointment.
Wo asked in our issuo of June 2nd, “ What 1s to prevent
the Department selecting five Britishers?’ And time
has answered that question. Simply nothing prevented
the wiso Department, whosoe injured innocent_head is
now being whitewashed, from doing so; and they did
it. The Department sent on the names of five Britishers
to tho Waterford Committee, and from these five flaxen-
haired Saxons or dour Wha-hae’s, the local committee
were to select their choice. And if a Sandy were selected
and there were a rumpus, the injured-innocent Sir
Horaeo would turn round and say— Please, gentlemen,
turn your abuse on the members of the Waterford Com-
mit{ee who made the appointment.’’

‘We hear that the name of one of the five, a Mr. Fer-
guson, was underlined by the Department. The Water-
ford Committee had indeed nominated this gentleman,
when Dr. O’Sullivan and Father Furlong very properly
protested that an applicant by the name of O’Shaugh-
nessy, a well qualified man, had not been considered.
Eventually it was decided to expressly ask the Depart-
ment if Mr. O’Shaughnessy was qualified. On Satur-
day last the reply came by wire from the Department
that “the Committeo may proceed with O’Shaughnessy
election.” The matter rests now with the local men,
though why, if Mr. O’Shaughnessy is eligible, he was
not included in the Department’s original five is one of
those questions upon which the members of the public
will no doubt exercise their private judgment—the in-
fallibility, not to say the impeccability, of the Depart-
ment notwithstanding.

The meeting of the Waterford Technical Committee
will be held on Friday, June 15, and we hope that the
members, both clerical and lay, will see that fair play
is given to the Irish applicant who has been pronounced
eligible. ~ We wonder were there any other eligible
Irishmen excluded from the Department’s most careful
selection of five assorted Britishers? However, now
that even one ‘Irishman is pronounced eligible we hope
his claims will be carefully considered by the members
of the Committee at Friday’s meeting.

‘What is the nationality of the agnicultural machinery
used at the Department’s agricultural stations? We
have been told that at one station the total amount of
Irish manufactured agricultural machinery is repre-
sented by one root-pulper. Perhaps those of our readers
who possess information about these matters would com-
municate with us.

At a recent. weekly meeting of the Cork Coiroe
- Ceanncaip of the Gaelic League, the Coipoe represent-
ing eight branches in the district formally repudiated
the statements made by Mr. Augustine Roche on  tho
floor of the House,” that the peoplo of Cork city and
district were opposed to having licensed premises closed
at 9 p.m. on Saturday nights, and for the ontire day
on Sundays, and for extending the ‘“ bona *’ limit, They
condemned the action of both “Mr. Augustine Roche
and myself ** for voting against the Bill brought in by
Mr. Sloan. The Coipoe did this, though they—and
we share their view—believe more in appealing to the
patriotism and self-respect of the Irish people for tem-
perance reform than in legislation. 1t is plain,
however unpalatable the fact may be, that appeals
to patriotism and self-respect where the profits of Mu.
Bung are concerned will not carry us all the way. The
almost complete collapse of the voluntary effort to close
Bung on St. Patrick’s Day in Dublin has clearly proved
that much. By the way, would it not be well if some
body or other—we think the Church Street organisation
would suit very well—passed a resolution expressing ap-
proval of the Bill before the British Parliament, and
sent it round to all the Urban and Rural Councils in
the country?

24 The Committee of the Keating Branch of the Gaelic
League passed a resolution registering their view that
the refusal of the Local Government Board to sanction
the appointment of Seamup O'Dein as medical
officer at Oughterard is calculated to seriously impede
the language movement. If the L.G.B. care to persist
in their attitude towards this case it may be all the
better, for it will be a good case to fight out with them,
and the whole force of the Gaclic League should be
called up to the encounter.

There was a special meeting of the Limerick County
Agricultural and Technical Education Committee called
to consider the reply to be sent in writing by invitation
to the Royal Commission to whitewash the Department
(Scotch).  Monsignor Hallinan, of Newcastle West,
was unable to attend, but he wrote a most interesting
letter. Monsignor Hallinan is one of the most earnest
and sincere Irish Irishmen in the country, and anything
he says is worthy of attention, however others may dis-
agree with his views. It will be remembered that he
threw himszlf whole-heartedly into the fight for Toler-
ance, Justice and Fair Play on the Great Sourface Rail-
way, and he is a thorough-going Gaelic Leaguer. In
the course of his letter he wrote:—

As regards the working of the Department of Agriculture and
Technical Instruction since its promotion, as far as I can judge,
it has, I believe, failed to realise the hopes that we entertained
of its usefulness to the couniry. As far as my oxperience goes,
I see Little evidence of much practical good to the country from
its operations. I gee no improvemont in our system of agricul-
ture here in Limerick, the technical education given is of an
itinerant kind, and does some little good, but nothing commen-
surate with the amount of money cxpended on it. No cffort of
any kind has been made to start or cncourage local industries—
in fact the officials of the Department declaro that this is not
their business, hence it has effected nothing towards stopping
emigration. The only two girls’ technical schools in this county
are here and at Abbeyfealo, and bots n both we have not as yet
gol more than £70 or £80, whilst hundreds are being spent in
payiLg officials, Officialism and red-tapoism havo from tho first
blighted its prospects. No cffort has been made to study the
ideals or needs of this country, but, as in most other depart-
ments, they have taken their ideals and systems from othor coun-
tried quite different from this, and imagined that what suits thom
ought to suit us. Then, a huge blunder was mado from the
start by th's wholesale importation of Scolchmen and Englishmen
to man the Department, who, however compefont and worlhy,
could not be expected to know the requirements of this country.
That it was necessary to import some cannot bo deniod, but what
should have only baen done sparingly and provisionally becamo
u seitled policy. They made the Department unpopular from
the start, and poople who were otherwise disposed to give it a
fair chance and co-operato with it got disgusted. It came to be
regarded very soon as another buttress of Dublin Castlo, @ now
detachment of a foreign garrison. 1In fact, as far as I can form
an opinion, a sweeping chango is required in its spirit, ils con-
stitution, and methods. My exparience is limiled to this counlry
(7county), and honoe my opinion may bo of little value.

our monthly contemporary, the Irish Rosary, for
- Juno, & paragraph headed “ He Must Go '’ refers to Sir

‘THE LEADER.

Horace Plunkett as Vice-President of the Department.
Our contemporary says:—

A ocouplo of mouths ago I expressed doubt as to the real pur-
poso for which the Committeo of Inquiry was formed. ~There is
no doubt whatover now. Its clear intention is to apply a Lh'ml(
couting of whitewash to tho Vice-Prosident and the other officials
of tho Department. It may even try to do something more. It
may have the face to recommend that Sir Horaco 'Pluukolt be
retained in the Department in defiance of public opinion.

There is certainly a pleasant diversity of opinion on
many matters now, and this fact indicates a growth of
individuality ; the days when a political organisation
turned a handle, and brought forth opinions for uni-
versal national consumption are happily gone.

Whilst tho Zrish Rosary is of opinion that any
attempt to keep Sir Horace in his position should be
“ fiercely rosisted,”” his Lordship the Bishop of Ross is
almost as emphatic the other way. During his evidence
before the Inquiry Dr. Kelly said : —

I don’t think there is any man in Ireland I would more prefer

to work with than the present Vice-Prosident, and if the Liberal
Government wished to re-appoint him and leave him in the same
position as he has been in from 1900, wo would all be very
salisfied.
Well, we do not think the country would be satisfied.
No doubt you could jump from the frying pan into the
firo by appointing a more undesirable man than Sir
Horace; the need is to appoint a better one, and one
in whose practical working capacity the country will
have more confidence.

We sce that Mr. Stephen Gwynn has been lecturing at
Granard, in the County of Longford. In the course of
his lecture he stated that probably most of his audience.
were Catholics. Hundreds of Protestants, he said,
were feeling that those who could not take the Irish
point of view would be strangers te Ireland. We are
glad to hear that ; it is about time that a change of that
kind did take place. But what are hundreds but a few
drops in the ocean of the upwards of a million Protes-
tants in Ireland? When will thousands, and tens and
hundreds of thousands, begin to feel like that?  Mr.
Gwynn informed his audience that it would be a great
pity if we were to try and use the word “Irish’’ in a
sense that should exclude anybody who was willing to
work with us. We agree. But what in the name of all
that’s sensible is the use in uttering such a warning?
Who now, or at any past time, suggested that anyone
should be excluded from being Irish if he were willing to
be of Ireland? We often state the general fact which
is perhaps oftener emphasised by the Orange Party that
the Catholics are the Irish, and the non-Catholics are
the England’s Faithful Garrison. Unfortunately we
believe there are far more Sourface Catholics than
there are Irish-Treland Protestants. But no one warns
Protestants off the Irish grass; they can come on, and
welcome; but they don’t come. Perhaps it is
“ bigoted *’ to open your eyes and state what you see.

Mr. Gwynn, with rather doubtful taste, we think,
said that “ between the Gaelic League and the Catholic
clergy in certain places there was a conflict arising.”’ Now
what is that to him? Ho is quite free, of course, as Sir
Horace Plunkett was, to discuss any matter he likes;
but would it not come better from him, a Protestant,
if he went round to Christian Young Men’s Associations,
and told them of the Gaelic League, and how there was
no conflict between his parsons and the League, for that
with a very odd excoption his parsons quite ignored the
League, and kept it at a distance. When some Protes-
tants condescend to become Irish they appear to be
unable to restrain their desire to give the ‘“unen-
lightened ’  Papists tho benefit of their wise advice.
There has been some friction with individual Catholic
priests, and we think it has been made far too much
of ; and certainly the “ hundreds’’ of Protestants who
may have como to see that there is some good in Irish
Iroland might, at least, keop out of the way. M
Gwynn, according to the press report, said:—
* Novertheless tho fack romained that up and down throughou
Ircland thero was a good deal of friction between the G&e
League and the Catholio clergy. Now he wished to shoy

o st place that the clergy were doing their cause great

jury when they opposed a movement that had been so highly

and so justly landed by the Hierarchy of tho Catholic Church.

He thought tho reason was they had beon for a long time in
exclusive control almost.- Thoy had had authority forced upon

~ them, and now when they saw a new power growing up in their
mld.sc'\‘.hey looked on it with somo suspicion. When a gontloman
<ocaupied a position of authority he had power to mako an immenso
deal of mischief, if he was not overwiso and discrect.

One would have thought that tho history of Sir Horaco
Plunkett’s literary excursions into matters concerning
the Catholics and their clergy would have taught discre-
tion to others of his creed. After saying what wo have
quoted, Mr. Gwynn remarked :—“ Howover, it is not
his business to speak to the clergy.”” Then why did ho
do so? Let him speak to the parsons and the  Irish-
men *’ of the Young Men’s Christian Associations. Bub
Mr. Campbell, M.P., who said that there was no
““ cash’ in Irish, would be more welcome in these circles,

Mr. Concannon, the chief organiser of the Gaelic
League, is back safe in Ireland again. He is looking
fit, and is in the best of spirits with himself and the
world.  Tho mission of An Cpaobin to the United
States was a surprising success, and though Dr. Hyde
has a way with him and an enthusiasm that are calcu-
lated to take audiences by storm, yet he would be the
first to admit, we are sure, that a deal of the success of
his great mission to America is due to the organising
ability of Tomap L4&n.  Themoral effect of the recent
campaign in America must be, on the face of it, consi-
derable and far-reaching, yet the measurement of it is
a matter of conjecture. But one tangible fact with
length, breadth, and thickness is that as a result of the
mission the Gaelic League is tho richer by about ten

.thousand pounds. Now, it is not exactly a simple sum,
but it is a sum in proportion—If two men go out to
America on behalf of the Gaelic League and bring back

b ten thousand pounds in filthy lucre, what is the extent
of the moral effect which they have produced in Ame-
rica in relation to Ireland? Ten thousand pounds for
what in America appeals to people almost wholly as a
sentimental movement takes a lot of getting, and behind
the figures it is not difficult to discern the vague out-
lines of the enormous impression which Dr. Hyde’s mis-
sion created.

Tomar Dan, now that he is back in Ireland, can stand
in any Irish Ireland assembly with the comfortable feel-
ing that as one of the envoys to America he was
associated with something attempted, something done.
We are very glad to hear, and we are sure our readers
will be glad to hear, that Mr. Concannon is to be mar-

ried next month. His future wife, Miss Walsh, is a
% sister of Mr. Louis Walsh, solicitor, of Maghera, whoso
name and ability are well known in Irish Ireland circles.
‘Whatever may have been said of the recent marriage of
the King of Spain, certainly the forthcoming marriage
of Mr. Concannon will be one of the chief social events
of the year in Irish Ireland. We offer him our congra-
tulations and best wishes, and we are sure that we might
do likewise on the part of all our readers.

His lordship the Bishop of Armidale was present at
the celebration of the feast of St. John Baptist de la
Salle at the de la Salle College in Waterford. During
an interval in tho proceedings Mr. Thomas Asho read
an address of wolcome to Bishop O’Connor. Tho Bishop
of Armidale replied in Irish and in English. His lord-
ship, in tho course of his remarks in English, said:—
“T deem it a shame for an Irishman to have to admit
that he is ignorant of his nation’s tongue.. And to mo
indeed it is most satisfactory that owing to the offorts of
real patriots it will soon again be heard in tho market-
place, the school, aye, and I hope, too, from the pulpit.”

We see that a British firm are advertising a drink
called “stym.”” Tt is some sort of hop bitters. ~Wo
" have a label before us, and upon the label we read
~ amongst other things, “Bottled by J. G. Mooney and
" Co., Ltd.” We wonder have theso Mooneys yet con-
descended to stock Irish-made hop bitters? If not, is
it not a real injustice to the industrial interests of Ire-
nd? Perhaps Mr. Mooney, M.P., would ask a question

the House of Commons, and if he does not get a
gatisfactory answer from the Chiof Secretary, at least

the question will draw public attention to the matter.

We hear on good authority that there is not as much
as a hottlo of Irish cider in oune of Mooney’s shops,
though ono of the directors of the company that runs
thom is a rcal live “Nationalist’’ M.P., and Mr.
Gerald Mooney is one of tho directors of the Freeman’s
Journal. Mooneys have two or three shops in London,
and London Irishmen who drink cider should be able
to mako their practical patriotism felt in these places.
Moonoys, wo believe, stock American and Devonshire
cider, and they spunged on St. Patrick by opening on
the 17th March. It is all very well for young Mooney
to be a ““tried and true’’ on the “ floor of the House,””
but why does ho not see that Irish cider is stocked in
his shops? Irish cidor is now made—and the makers
aro ot Dark Brothers either—by the Co. Armagh Cider
Co., by J. H. Rutter and Co., Cork, and by Mr. Thos.
Power, Dungarvan.

The new Temperancoe Club at Corbally, near the Kil-
laloo slate quarries, was opened last week by the Bishop
of Killaloe, Most Rev. Dr. Fogarty. The Bishop deli-
vered a practical address. He naturally had a good
word for the slates, and all we can say is that in the
interests of Irish industrial development we wish that
tho directors of the company were half or quarter as
good as the slates. Dr. Fogarty asked:—* As long as
wo have here on Irish soil good Irish slates, cut by Irish
hands from the ribs of an Irish mountain, why go with
Irish money to a foreign market?’ We hope the man-
agement has been shaken up, and that the director who
got the gold medal for mathematics in his youth is not
living on that particular item of youthful fame, but is
endeavouring to earn new fame as a competent and
useful director. You can’t, even in this country, be-
come immortal on the strength of a gold medal, the
while you neglect even to insure your boiler.  Father
Donnellan, P.P., pointed out that almost every house
in the neighbourhood was concerned either directly or
indirectly in the working of the quarry. The new hall
certainly should add to the comfort and efficiency of
the quarrymen; and we hope that the honest work of
these men will not in any way be thwarted by ineffi-
ciency on the part of the directors of the quarry.

There are so many Commissions and Inquiries going
on now that one would almost have to suspend his ordi-
nary business, limit his sleep to four hours, and take
his lunch out of a paper parcel if he wanted to keep up
with them. We see they are inquiring into the Belfast
police system. One witness, Sergeant Kerrigan, said
that the “ good duty list’” was monopolised by men of
a certain denomination, whereupon Commissioner Sin-
gleton said such recriminations were a disgrace to the
Belfast police force. Of course! If a Papist complains
of bigotry it is disgraceful! Sergeant Kerrigan was
proceeding to give a specific case of what he complained,
but was advised not to go into details! They don't
want details. One Mr. Job Cherry, a P.L.G., and evi-
dently a “true blue,”’ declared, “TIt is the generally
provalent opinion that the Catholic clergy control the
police force.”” Poor old clergy, what next we wonder?
Perhaps it was the Catholic clergy who forced Edward
VII. to swear that they and theirs were all « Idolators !’
We do not read that Commissioner Singleton pulled up
Job Cherry; he evidently showed great patience with
Job. Tt was apparently left to Mr. J. T. Donaghy, an
“Idolatrous ’ solicitor, to protest, and thereupon Job
counter protested against being thus “ chastised !’

A lady writes us that she was discussing with a friend
tho forthcoming garden party at Cork. It will be re-
membered that Lady Aberdeen is to be present, and that
sho expressed a wish that all the visitors, both men and
women should be dressed in clothes of Irish manufac-
ture. Our correspondent’s friend’s difficulty is that so
far as she knows nothing sufficiently light and ‘‘dressy’’
looking for such an event is being presented to the Cork
ladies by any of the warchouses. What are the Cork
shops doing? People will attend this function, and
they will be looking, we suppose they are now looking,
for suitable dresses, etc., of Irish manufacture. Besides
the beautiful dress materials made at the Dripsey mills,
and the Foxford Industry, there are, we believe, various

for suitable Irish manufactured stuff, and could not
get it.

The terrible exposures of American canned meat pack-
ing methods which have been shocking the public for
some time past should not be without a beneficial effect
upon the Irish meat trade. They should indeed have a
beneficial effect on Irish industry generally by way of
giving a final knock down to the unreasonable and un-
patriotic prejudice against Irish goods, s‘mply because
they are Irish, which obtains in some quarters in Ire-
land, and against which we claim ourselves to have
performed some telling work. In view of the American
revelations a visit to an Irish bacon factory possesses
just now a special interest. A number of Press repre
sentatives on Tuesday last were shown through the ex-
tensive factory of Messrs. Kehoe, Donnelly, Ltd , Dub-
lin, where the manager and directors explained in
detail the various processes of their manufacture, from
the killing of the pig to its transformation iuto sau-
sages and puddings. Large quantities of foreign sau-
sages are, we understand, imported into Ireland from
England principally, and it is rumoured that American
semi-raw material is sometimes used in the manufacture
of sausages there. Messrs. Kehoe, Donnelly of course
use only Irish pork in their manufacture. It
is a favourable comment on this firm’s manu-
facture that they export tho bulk of their hams
to Paris, where they command a much higher
price than they do in Ireland, and we are informed
that their exports to France could be largely increased
did the support they get at home for middles, etc., 4
justify an increase of purchase of pigs. The public R
will be glad to learn that the firm has had a good 5
half year’s trading, and is looking forward to further
dovelopment in the near future.

DEaR Sir—I regret to have to inform you that, judg-
ing from your paragraph under the heading of “ Cur-
rent Affairs,”’ in your issue of 2nd inst., you have been
wrongly informed regarding the action taken by this 3
branch of the above society in the matter of excursions
of the sodalities and other organisations of this city for

D. W. and W., as the chairman (or, as you term him,
the “ Grand Steward ’ of the Freemasons in Ireland),
or any other subordinate official of the G. S. and W.
had not in any manner of form influenced such action
as taken by the members of this branch, which had been
taken from a trades union point: of view only. As to your
comment on the reply from Father Furlong, it may

- have been very proper, from a religious point of view,

but for the exposure of the D. W. and W, for the very
samo fault in the House of Commons by Mr. Jeremiah
M‘Veagh, M.P., by his giving notice of his intention to
oppose the D. W. and W. Bill unless an undertaking
was given that clerkship appointments should be made
only after, and as a result of, competitive examinations.
And to my mind, so long as the Catholic clerks and
officials of the G. 8. and W. or any other railway com-
pany dopend upon outside aid for the redress of their °
grievances, and remain inactive and indifferent them-
selves to such harsh treatment as alluded to by Father
Furlong, so long shall such exist. Trusting that you
shall be able to accommodate us by publication liereof,
I remain, yours truly,

M. O’Connogr,
Branch Secretary.

We at once accept the correction that the move in
favour of the Great Sourface Railway as against the
D.W. & W. did not come officially from employés of the
Great So‘urface. Railway as such,but from employés cf that
railway in their capacity as members of a combination of
railway sexrvants of England, Iveland, Scotland
Wales. In whatover capacity they moved the
point, it seems to us, is that the deputation, or wha
over it was, wished amongst other things that “Idola
trous  sodalities should give a preference to the

~ the British Parliament! Bub what about the question

- Sourface Railway, the Chairman of which is “ Grand
‘Steward *’ of the Freemasons in this country. We ro-
peab that in our opinion Father T. F. Furlong,
Administrator of the Cathedral, very properly replied
that he could not entertain any resolutions giving pre-

. ference to the G. S. and W. over tho D. W. and w.,

- owing to the unfair treatment of Catholics in tho G- S.
and W. The members of the deputation, or whatever it
was, approached “Idolatrous’’ sodalitics not in their
capacity as employés of the Great Sourfaco Railway,
bub in their capacity as members of an amalgamation
of railway servants of England, Scotland, Wales and Tre-
land, the head offices of which are in London, and tho
telegraphic address of which is, appropriately enough,
“‘ Beware, London.”’” Wo are not sure in what particu-
lar capacity Father Furlong acted, but we assume ho
-acted as spokesman of some Catholic sodality or socicty
that was approached; and just as tho very much amali-
.gamated deputation could take no cognisance of bigotry,
nationality or anything petty of that sort, so wo take it
that Father Furlong, being approached as a member of,
or as one in authority in a Catholic sodality, naturally
looked at the matter from the Catholic sodality’s point
of view.

It is news to us to learn of any exposure of bigotry
in the D. W. and W. There may be bigotry there for
all we know, and if there is we would be glad to get
particulars of it for publication. We have not read that
Mr. MVeagh charged the D. W. and W. with bigotry ;
we have read that he used his influence to induco the
company to throw open their clerkships to competitive
examination. Catholics in this country demand nothing
but Tolerance, Justice and Fair Play; and if it were
shown that every clerk in a railway company was a
-Catholic, we, and all other liberal and tolerant Catholics
like us, would give our support to a proposal to throw
open the clerkships to competitive examinations.
Upon what aid other than outside aid—the aid of the
press, of public opinion, of the sharcholders and the
customers of the railway—could the Catholic clerks of
the Great Sourface Railway, chairmanned by the “Grand
Steward *’ of the Freemasons in Ireland, depend for re-
«dress of bigotry? Perhaps they might, in an heroic
moment, have started a Great Sourface Railway
‘Clerks’ Defence Society; but there would be many
<dangers and difficulties besetting such a move. All the
.amalgamated societies of English, Scotch, Welsh and
Irish people would probably protest against—as the
loose phrase goes—“ dragging religion into business.”
Why, our correspondent’s friends, as members of the
English-Scotch-Welsh-Irish amalgamation, would be
Jjumped on if they attempted to act as a body as Catho-
lics, or even as Irishmen; they are only a chip of the
international organisation whose telegraphic address is
““ Beware, London.”” Why do not these Irish railway
servants beware of London, and start a trades union of
their own?

ration of Irish Trades’ Societics; and Mr, Greig, of
‘Belfast, as an amendment, proposed afliliation with tho

wag Home Rule as against United Kingdom Rule; and
it is not surprising that Mr. Greig, of Belfast, was on

““ Unionist *’ arguments in favour of his view: ‘‘Tho

9 HE active Ivish Trelander must, I think, if ho

have a sense of humour, often smile to himself,
must, if he have not a sense of humour, often swear to
himself, must, in any case, often scratch his head in
bewilderment at the sort of Irishmen he meets, and the
sort of arguments about Irish and Irish Ireland that he
hears. I am only moderately active myself, but I am
-continually finding myself in situations that suggest this
reflection. For instance, I sit as I write between an
Irish Nationalist and a Frenchman. The former is hav-
ing his children taught French (in such manner as
French is taught in Ireland), on the grounds that it is
a commercial language, which Irish, though all right
from a sentimental point of view, is not, and never
will be. The Frenchman, however, is not having his
-children taught French, nor can they speak it—he says
there is nothing to be made out of it in this country!
Again, several other people with whom I have had argu-
ments about Irish Ireland, people who are above using
so common and unpatriotic an argument as the commer-
-cial one, have thrown it in my face that we Irish Ire-
landers are altogether too insular and narrow-minded, if
we were a bit broad-minded we would be all right—the
fact being, of course, that it is they and their likes who
are insular and narrow-minded inasmuch as they would
not touch anything Zrish with a forty-foot pole. (By
the way, there is surely some excuse for a person not
choosing to cotton to the stranger; there can be very
little for a person disdaining his own!) Yet again I have
been put the poser by more than one not unsympathetic
doubter of my sanity—if there is any good, they have
asked, in Irish literature, or ever was, why did not such
great scholars and such impartial men as, for instance,
Gladstone and John Morley, know Irish or want to learn
it? And so on. I suppose many of my readers have
had a similar experience to mine. Taking it for
granted that they have, I find myself, in pessimistic
moments, marvelling that there is so little pessimism
usually to be found in the pages of the Lraper or the
-other Irish-Ireland papers. At times, too, in Irish-
Ireland gatherings, with the strains “Sinn pén”
in my ears, and the waves of Sinn {éin enthusiasm
‘buoyant about my heart and head and brain, the thought
comes to me—Who are Sinn pPéin —and T find myself
asking myself—If we, Irish-Trelanders, comprise " Sinn
Féin,” what about the rest of the country?

I have been saying recently in the LeapEr that wo
need not expect that many of the present grown genera-
tion of Irishmen will ever learn Irish. I did not say,
‘however, that we need despair of making a good propor-
tion of them Irish Irelanders in all other respects. This
latter is what a good many enthusiastic Gaels soem to
think. I mean those who would boycott, and thereby
practically shut the gate against those of their country-
men who play foreign games, dance foreign dances, and
50 on. I don’t wish to speak dogmatically, but I doubt
‘the wisdom of drawing up the draw-bridge yet a bit,
After all, it is not so very long since all of us played,
or were partial to, foreign games, danced or enjoyed

foreign dances, sang or applauded foreign songs, and so
on. Similarly many—nay, most of those of us who feel
inclined to denounce angrily as unpatriotic the Irish
emigrant for emigrating—have ourselves run out of the
<ountry, and would do so again in the morning under

~ the circumstances that originally caused us to run, I

don’t think I am overstating. If we have, as we think,
found salvation, each individual one of us knows how
almost altogether a matter of chance it was in his own
case that he did find it, and the knowledge ought to
temper tho severity of his criticism of those who have
not yet found it. The finding salvation doesn’t do
away with the obligation to be charitable. Besides,
although the orthodox Irish Irelander may not logically
tolerato what ho believes to be false principles, he has
no right to be intolerant of opinions, no matter how
falso ho may hold them to be. Those Irishmen to whom
I have referred as amusing and bewildering me with
their questions about, and objections to, Irish Ireland,
I belicvo to hold wrong opinions on the subject, but all
the samo I don’t feel called upon to insult them, or
oven to shun their company. It has taken more than
a hundred years to produce the Ireland and the Irish-
man of to-day; I don’t think a dozen years sufficient to
undo the work of more than a century.

The reader will, I hope, apprehend the train of
thought that has led me to these somewhat disjointed
reflections. But in a sentence of which I have before
made use, that thought is, that the Irish-Ireland con-
viction has, as yeb, scarcely penetrated the skin of the
Irish body politic, and that therefore, however strongly
wo Irish Irelanders may feel that we are right, and that
only our way lies salvation for Ireland, it is a bit prema-
ture yet to call ourselves “Sinn pém,” or, at least,
to lay down the national law, and ban and boycott the
rest of our countrymen accordingly. As I have said,
ono needs an all-round humour to be enabled to take a
philosophic view of latter-day, topsey-turvey Ireland.
Just at present, too, there are so many proposals for our
betterment in the field, and the sponsors of those pro-
posals so strenuous-looking standing by them, it is no
casy matter to pick one’s steps publicly without stand-
ing on someone’s toes. Writing as one who has toes of
his own, I beg, not to add any new proposal to those
referrzd to, but to pass a few remarks relative to one or
two matters that have recently been under discussion in
connection with the Sinn Fém policy. I do so with the
same idea with which John Mitchel, on board the
¢ Scourge,”’ wrote out the Politeia “in literal, bold
English ;" that is, T wish to satisfy myself that I under-
stand what exactly we mean by this Sinn Féin move-
ment, and what are the immediate possibilities of it.

To start with, I suppose the Sinn Féin man’s ideal
Ireland is an Irish Ireland. If that be granted, it
follows, I think, that the Irish-Ireland party includes
the Sinn péin party. So much further being granted—
and bearing in mind, as I have said above, that the
Irish-Treland idea has as yet scarcely penetrated the
skin of the Irish body politic—I suggest that we can’t
get away from the conclusion that the Sinn pém
party is but a very small fraction of even the Nationalist
Ireland of the present day. The question, then, is—on
what lines that small fraction had better work if it
hopes to attain its end—the freeing and the Irishising
of the whole Irish people?

I have, for convenience, spoken of the Sinn Fémn
party as one, and as being included in the Irish-Ireland
party. This, however, is not sufficiently accurate.
Less broadly speaking the Sinn péin party is composed
of two divisions—one lod by the LEADER, the other by
“Smn_ pém.”  The end of whose two parties is the
same, but they seek it along different roads. The
LeApER party, recognising facts, says that to work the
Sinn pém policy you must have a people who will re-
cogniso themselves as Sinn  Péin—that is a people with
a backbone; it therefore works to put a backbone into
the people. Thoe other party, on the contrary, seems to
hold that you can work the Sinn péin policy with any
sort of people. The former holds that it is the people
that make theSinn {'éin (or any other) policy possible;
the latter scems to hold that it is the Sinn Féin policy

SIR—I was surprised to read in your issue of 2nd
inst. that, of 96 bicycles used to carry their riders to a
Gaelic football match held lately near Mullingar, only
two bicycles were of Irish make.

It would be interesting to know where the hats and
boots and cloth in the coats worn by these patriots were
manufactured ; but I shrewdly suspect it was far from
Mullingar.

I enclose you a few lines to emphasise the significance
of the auspicious event—if you think worth insertion.—
Yours truly, Mac.

8th June, 1906.

(Air, “ The Irish Jaunting Car,’”’ with apologies to the
shade of Valentine Vousden).

Does your honour want a bicycle?
Sure here’s the one for you.

A true loyal one of British build,
All painted red and blue.

It looks so bright an’ runs so tight,
And travels firm an’ far;

Sure ’tis what the Gaelic athletes use
In gallant Mullingar.

So buy a raal English one,
Frame, tyre an’ axle-tree,

An’ spokes all made by Johnny Bull
In British Coventree.

Rudge-Whitworth, Humber, Enfield,
Sparkbrook, or Royal Star—

All Britain’s best an’ used the most
In gallant Mullingar!

And if you want a bargain,
Sure you haven’t far to seek,

We’ll give you sportin’ British terms,
Just eighteenpence a week.

’Tis cheaper far than ould Shank’s mare,
Or Larry Doolin’s car;

’Tis what the Gaelic athletes use
In gallant Mullingar!

His heart is warm, his spirit high,
His Gaelic foothall true;

An’ (if he strikes a foreign match)
Sure.so do others too;

* An Irishman, from top to toe,

Deny it if you dar!

But his bicycle is built far, far
From gallant Mullingar!

HAT the Act of 1723 took the payment for the
Exhibitions as part of the Trust imposed by
Erasmus Smith, is also proved (a) by the fact that the
Act has given legal power to lessen the number of Ex-
hibitions, or the sums paid for each, in case the rents
should grow less. It says:—‘‘ And in like manner, if
at any time . . . hereafter, the yearly rents . . . of
the said lands . . . shall decrease or grow less, That,
then, it shall and may be lawful to, and for, the said
Governors, . . . and they are hereby Impowered to
lessen the number of Pensions or Exhibitions of the said
College, or to make such deductions . . . of all or any
of the Pensions, Exhibitions, as they shall think fit.””
There should be no need of Impowering the Governors
to make deductions in the Pensions if the Legislature
did not take them as part of the Trust. Therefore, the
Legislature did take them as part of the Trust.

That the Legislature did take the payment for the
Exhibitions as part of the Trust is also proved (b) by
the words:—‘‘ And whereas the said Erasmus Smith
charged all and every the lands . . . set apart as afore-
said with the several annual sums hereinafter men-
tioned.”” Among the ‘‘sums hereinafter mentioned ’”
are the payments for the Exhibitions. Therefore, the
Legislature took it for granted that those payments had
been imposed by Erasmus Smith as part of the Trust.
Therefore, the Act of 1723 gave the Governors no legal
power to pay for those Exhibitions to Trinity College;
and, as the payment was illegal and a breach of Trust
before the Act of 1723, it remained illegal and a breach
of Trust after it.

Nor can it be maintained that the Governors have
got any legal power to make those payments for Ex-
hibitions to Trinity College from the following words
of the Act:—‘ And be it further enacted by the
authority aforesaid, that the 20 several yearly Pensions
or Exhibitions of £8 a piece, and the 15 several Pen-
sions . . . of £6 a piece, hereinbefore mentioned to
have been paid out of the said lands to poor scholars of
the said College, shall from time to time, and at all
times hereafter, be paid and payable out of the rents
and profits of the said lands . . . to the like number of
scholars, members, or students of the said College, re-
spectively, ¢ill they shall be otherwise provided for,
removed, or take the Degree of Master of Arts in the
said College, or shall be of standing to take such Master
of Arts Degree.”’

For this quotation either gives legal power to the
Governors to make those payments for Exhibitions to
Trinity College, or fixes the time during which those
Exhibitions are to continue for individual students in
Trinity College. ~ We cannot take the Act as giving
legal power to the Governors to pay for those Exhibi-
tions to Trinity College, for I have already proved that
the Act and the Legislature took it for granted, that
the Governors had that power already. Therefore, we
must take the Act and the Legislature as merely fixing

That they are breaches of Trust is also cvident from
two facts: —1. No stranger in Trinity College is entitled
to hold one of those Exhibitions so long as there is a

2. If the Endowment were made available for the
Catholic tenants there should be hundreds of tenants’

Now I shall expose the great fraud which has been
executed, and is being executed, by this Act of 1723.
It has been obtained from the Legislature by a most
outrageous and shameful suppression of truth, and as-
sertion of falsehood ; and it has been applied to execute
purposes for which it has given no legal power, and to
which it was never intended by the Legislature to
apply. g

This, briefly, is the fraud: —The Governors told the
Legislature that they had carried out all the Trusts im-
posed on the Endowment by Erasmus Smith; that,
after they had carried into effect all the Trusts imposed
by Erasmus Smith, they had a surplus on hand. Both
these statements were false. They had not carried into
effect all the Trusts; and they had a surplus on hand,
Decause they had not done so. They asked the Legisla-
ture for legal power to apply that alleged surplus to
certain specified objects. The Legislature gave them
legal power to apply that alleged surplus to the speci-
fied objects. .

The Governors left nearly all the Trusts, imposed on
the Endowment by Erasmus Smith, unfulfilled ; called
the money which remained on hand, because they had
not fulfilled, or carried into efiect those Trusts, a sur-
plus; and applied it to the objects they had _spcclﬁcd,
They had no legal authority whatever for this course.
Because, except for £5 a year to be given to a scln_oo]~
master in Bluecoat Hospital, the Legislature had given
them only legal power to dispose of the alleged surplus,
and had strictly defined the surplus’’ to be what re-
mained after all the Trusts, and charges in connection
with them, had been discharged. As the whole Endow-
ment would not be sufficient to discharge all the Trusts,
there could be no surplus as defined by the Legislature.
Therefore there was no money, or could be no money,
to which the legal power of application given by the Act
of 1723 could apply. And, hence, the whole conduct
of the Governors, since 1723, in applying nearly all the
Endowment to objects different from those intended by
Erasmus Smith, has been illegal, and a breach o[' Trust,

By this legal fraud, the Governors have given to
Trinity College some £160,000; to Bluecoat Tospital,
some £180,000, or £200,000, and have wasted on the
English schools some £200,000, or £300,000. .

the legal power to dispose of the surplus; and that
logal power is precisely defined by precisely defining the
surplus. And we have the surplus most precisely de-
fined by this Act of 1723, to be “that rent which re- -
mains over and above the said yearly sums payable as
aforesaid, and all other necessary yearly payments and
charges, in and about the execution of the said Trust.””
The preambloe has told us what “the said yearly sums
payable as aforesaid” are. But what are all the
“ other necessary yearly payments and charges in and
about the execution of the said Trust?’ To answer
this question, we must find out what “ the said Trust ’*
is. Does it mean the Trust that is fully fulfilled by
the payment of “ the said yearly sums payable as afore-
said ¥’ TIts coming immediately after these sums, and
these sums having been put forward by the Governors,
as a full discharge of all the Trusts imposed on the
endowment, would seem to imply that it does. But
fortunately for the beneficiaries, the Act has, in two
other places, told us, in the most distinct terms, that it
doos not.

1. In the very beginning of the Act, and in the be-
ginning of the quotation in which the words “ the said
Trust ’’ occur, it says: “ Whereas Erasmus Smith did
formerly set apart several lands for the main-
tenance of schools and other chamtable uses.””  The
italics are mine. “ Other charitable uses ’’ clearly mean
all the other charitable uses, besides the maintenance
of the schools, which are mentioned in the three Instru-
ments of Foundation. And the “said Trust,” coming
aftor these words in the same passage, must mean and
include all the Trusts imposed on the Endowment by
tho Instruments of Foundation.

2. But that “‘the said Trust’ means and includes
all the Trusts imposed on the Endowment, by Erasmus
Smith, in his three Instruments of Foundation, is
stated in the most explicit terms immediately after-
wards in the Act. For after stating that it would be
“ greatly for the service of the public’ that two Lec-
tureships and three Fellowships should be established in
Trinity College, it adds: * Therefore, for the better
enabling the Governors of the said schools for the time
being, for ever hereafter to apply and dispose of the
surplus rents and profits of the said lands and tene-
ments, which shall annually remain after payment of
the several yearly sums already appointed and set apart
for charitable uses, as aforesaid, and all other necessary
charges in and about the execution of the Trusts re-
posed in them, to the uses herein mentioned.”” All the
Trusts imposed on the Endowment by Erasmus Smith
have been reposed in his Trusts, and, hence, “the
Trusts reposed in them,”” are all the Trusts imposed
by Erasmus Smith. Therefore, the surplus has been
defined by the words of the Act just quoted, to be “what
remains after payment of the aforesaid sums and all
other sums necessary for the carrying into effect all the
other Trusts imposed by Erasmus Smith.”” Thus the
Act has given no legal power to change any of the
Trusts imposed on the Endowment: it has only given
power to dispose of an alleged surplus, which, I shall
show in the next article, does not, and cannot, exist.
